title: Product-Led Growth Readiness

introduction.subtitle: Go-to-market readiness assessment. 6 min.
introduction.description:
Product-Led Growth Readiness helps you learn the vocabulary and typical expectations behind Time to Value, Activation & Self-Service, Product Signals & Data, Expansion & Virality. Use it as a private diagnostic: answer from observable evidence, not aspiration, and treat the result as a map for the next improvement cycle.

The score stays separate from the optional answer-support check, which asks whether your answers are based on opinion, anecdotes, documentation, or system-verified evidence. A high answer should mean the practice is visible, repeatable, and useful in real decisions.

This educational quiz runs entirely in your browser. You receive the baseline result without account creation or email.
introduction.duration: 8 diagnostic questions plus 4 evidence checks. Approximately 6 min.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Educational diagnostic only. Not legal, tax, financial, investment, medical, employment, regulatory, or psychometric certification advice.

questions.text: How quickly can a qualified new user experience meaningful value?

options.text: A. It happens rarely, reactively, or only when a crisis forces attention.
B. It happens occasionally, but timing depends on individual initiative or urgency.
C. There is a defined cadence with clear participants, owners, and triggers.
D. The cadence is frequent enough to change decisions, includes quality checks, and improves from outcomes.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Time to Value: How quickly can a qualified new user experience meaningful value
conceptHelp.summary: Time to Value means how quickly a qualified user reaches a meaningful first outcome after starting the product. This question narrows the concept to "How quickly can a qualified new user experience meaningful value?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Time to Value matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would trace "How quickly can a qualified new user experience meaningful value?" to customer behavior, product data, ownership, and a roadmap or go-to-market decision that changed because of it.

questions.text: How clearly is the first-value event defined?

options.text: A. This is not explicit yet in Time to Value; decisions depend on intuition, habit, or one-off judgement.
B. There is a plausible practice in Time to Value, but evidence, ownership, or repeatability is still partial.
C. Time to Value has a documented practice with examples, owners, and current evidence used in decisions.
D. Time to Value is reviewed, measured over time, and strong enough to guide trade-offs under pressure.
Not sure / not applicable.

options.text: A. The answer relies mainly on opinion, pitch, memory, or isolated examples.
B. There are supporting examples, but sources are partial, informal, or hard to compare.
C. There is documented evidence from relevant customers, operations, metrics, or records.
D. The evidence is triangulated, current, owner-reviewed, and strong enough to change a material decision.
Not sure / not applicable.

contextQuestions.text: What level of evidence supports your answers for Time to Value?

options.text: Opinion: mostly judgement, memory, or individual perception.
Anecdotal: examples exist, but they are not systematically documented.
Documented: there are current notes, models, policies, metrics, or records.
System-verified: evidence is routinely produced by a governed workflow, system, or review cadence.

conceptHelp.title: Time to Value
conceptHelp.summary: This question asks how well your answers about Time to Value are supported. Time to Value means how quickly a qualified user reaches a meaningful first outcome after starting the product. The goal is learning: separate what you know from what you suspect, remember, or still need to verify.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: A result about Time to Value is more useful when you know whether it rests on opinion, a few examples, current documents, or system-verified evidence. Weak support does not make the answer useless; it tells you what to learn next.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Choose the strongest support you actually have right now. Do not upgrade an answer because the practice feels important, and do not downgrade it if current records or systems really exist.
conceptHelp.example: If your Time to Value answer is based on a recent metric, policy, deal document, customer record, or operating review, choose documented or system-verified. If it is mostly memory or judgement, choose opinion or anecdotal.

questionStageTitle: Learn the concept, then answer from evidence
questionStageDescription: Use the concept button when a term is unfamiliar. Prefer observable evidence over aspiration or ideal self-description.
contextDescription: This step is not scored. It records what your diagnostic answers are based on: opinion, anecdote, documentation, or system-verified evidence. Use it to decide how much to verify the result before acting on it.
